coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots embedded polysulfone membranes for antibacterial performance in the process of forward osmosis
coredata.dc:creator: Mahat, Nur Akma
Shamsudin, Siti Aisyah
Jullok, Nora
Ma'Radzi, Akmal Hadi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials characterised by excellent hydrophilicity, simple synthesis, environment friendliness, low cost, and low toxicity. However, the extensive application of CQDs is limited by the toxicity and high cost of their precursors. Thus, the synthesis of CQDs with green materials is desired. In this work, we proposed a green synthesis of oil palm biomass-based CQD-derived activated carbon embedded into polysulfone-selective layers to develop thin-film composite membranes, denoted as CQDs-PSF, for forward osmosis. The effects of incorporating CQDs into the PSF membrane matrix and their performance in forward osmosis were examined. The physicochemical properties of the synthesised membranes with added CQDs nanoparticles (0.5%, 0.75%, and 1.0% CQD concentration) resulted in a porous and hydrophilic membrane. The membrane with 1.0% CQD concentration exhibited the highest water flux of 226 L/m2.h, and its reverse salt flux was 59.38 mol/m2 h. The antibacterial activities of the CQDs-PSF membranes were also assayed with the Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and the Gram-negative Escherichia coli. Results of bacterial diffusion tests of CQDs-PSF membranes indicated enhanced antibacterial activity. Overall, our findings proved that adding CQDs to PSF membrane enabled the effective enhancement of the water flux and improved antibacterial performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Forward osmosis
PSF membrane

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbonisation-activation process of oil palm biomass
Characterization of CQDs and membrane surface
Effect of CQDs on membrane intrinsic properties
Permeability of water and solute using CQDs-PSF membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQDs synthesised from oil palm biomass, (b) size distribution histogram obtained from TEM image inset lattice spacing d = 0.32 μm, (c) FTIR spectroscopy of CQDs and 1.0-CQDs-PSF.
(a) High-resolution XPS spectrum of CQDs-PSF membranes (b) C 1s spectrum (c) O 1s (d) S 2p
Cross-sectional SEM images of the membranes (a) PSF (b) 0.5-CQDs, (c) 0.75-CQDs, and (d) 1.0-CQDs.
(a) Water flux with different pressure (b) Reverse salt flux by FO process (c) Porosity.
Surface AFM images of (a) PSF membrane (b) 0.5-CQDs-PSF membrane (c) 0.75-CQDs-PSF and (d) 1.0-CQDs-PSF.
Diffusion disk test to analysis the antibacterial effect of PSF membrane and CQDs-PSF membrane towards (a) S. aureus gram positive and (b) E. coli gram negative bacteria.
The roughness average (Ra) and root mean square (Rms) of the CQDs-PSF membranes surface.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots embedded polysulfone membranes for antibacterial performance in the process of forward osmosis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Excellence for Biomass Utilization, Universiti Malaysia Perlis, Kompleks Pusat Pengajian Jejawi 3, 02600 Jejawi, Perlis, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Excellence for Biomass Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre of Excellence for Biomass Utilization, Universiti Malaysia Perlis, Kompleks Pusat Pengajian Jejawi 3, 02600 Jejawi, Perlis, Malaysia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Centre of Excellence for Biomass Utilization, Universiti Malaysia Perlis, Kompleks Pusat Pengajian Jejawi 3, 02600 Jejawi, Perlis, Malaysia.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Excellence for Biomass Utilization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials characterised by excellent hydrophilicity, simple synthesis, environment friendliness, low cost, and low toxicity. However, the extensive application of CQDs is limited by the toxicity and high cost of their precursors. Thus, the synthesis of CQDs with green materials is desired. In this work, we proposed a green synthesis of oil palm biomass-based CQD-derived activated carbon embedded into polysulfone-selective layers to develop thin-film composite membranes, denoted as CQDs-PSF, for forward osmosis. The effects of incorporating CQDs into the PSF membrane matrix and their performance in forward osmosis were examined. The physicochemical properties of the synthesised membranes with added CQDs nanoparticles (0.5%, 0.75%, and 1.0% CQD concentration) resulted in a porous and hydrophilic membrane. The membrane with 1.0% CQD concentration exhibited the highest water flux of 226 L/m2.h, and its reverse salt flux was 59.38 mol/m2 h. The antibacterial activities of the CQDs-PSF membranes were also assayed with the Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and the Gram-negative Escherichia coli. Results of bacterial diffusion tests of CQDs-PSF membranes indicated enhanced antibacterial activity. Overall, our findings proved that adding CQDs to PSF membrane enabled the effective enhancement of the water flux and improved antibacterial performance.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating global water crisis and energy shortage worldwide suggest the urgent need for alternative water sources [1]. The forward osmosis (FO) membrane process has attracted considerable attention for its applications in water purification [2], desalination [3], food processing [4], wastewater reuse, [5] and sustainable power generation [6]. However, the advancement of the FO membrane process is limited primarily by membrane fouling [7]. The three types of foulants are organic and inorganic precipitates and particulates [8]. The membrane performance will deteriorate owing to the foulant accumulation on the surface or within the pores of the membrane [9]. Many polymeric membranes suffer from fouling problems, directly affecting membrane performance with decreased water flux leading to increases in energy consumption and operation costs [10]. Therefore, mitigation of membrane-fouling resistance could be the key to enhancing the efficiency of membranes for FO applications. In most cases, fouled polymeric membranes are caused by surface hydrophilicity, surface charge, and surface roughness [11]. Therefore, improving surface hydrophilicity and roughness can effectively prevent foulant accumulation and deposition [12].

Various approaches to prevent fouling on polymer membrane surfaces have been suggested, involving surface modifications from chemical interactions, surface coating, plasma treatment, and blending with hydrophilic materials [10]. In recent years, nanoparticles, such as TiO2 [13], SiO2 [14], Al2O3 [15], ZrO2 [16] and Fe3O4 [17], have been incorporated into the membrane matrix, and the results suggest the effectiveness of membrane modification [18]. However, a high weight percentage of nanoparticles is required to effectively disperse them uniformly in the casting solution, which poses a considerable challenge.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), from carbon-based nanomaterials, recently have received much attention in membrane technology because they can improve membrane surface properties, leading to enhanced separation performance [19]. The production of CQDs is simple and cost-effective with their excellent physicochemical properties such as small size, good biocompatibility, high hydrophilicity, richness in surface functional groups, and low toxicity [20]. Moreover, the excellent chemical properties of CQDs, which contain high amounts of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups in their surfaces, lead to excellent water solubility and stability towards membrane formation, factors that are key to improving the membrane surface properties for separation processes [21].

The functional groups of CQDs, carboxyl and hydroxyl, can develop chemical bonds with other compounds to fabricate a substrate layer of matrix membranes [6]. CQDs can potentially be used to reduce the problems of hydrophobic membrane polymer materials, which have weak mechanical and physical properties, made from conventional polymers, such as polyvinylidene, polyacrylonitrile, and polysulfone. Therefore, the fabrication of CQDs-modified membranes has been explored by many researchers to optimise the membrane properties improving the performance in multiple ways for broad applications [6,10,22,23]. The application of CQDs from oil palm biomass as a modifier on membranes for water filtration applications has not been reported yet. These CQDs are inexpensive because of their biomass materials, compared to metal-organic compounds or graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), and can provide an active layer of membranes for improving separation process performance.

Furthermore, the surface functional groups of CQDs are key for fabricating membranes with excellent membrane properties and stability with good performance. Fathizadeh et al. have studied amine-modified CQDs using citric acid with ammonia to modify the polyamide matrix membrane [24]. The results indicate that terminal amine groups assisted the functional groups between CQDs and polyamide matrix thus allowing for the stable dispersion of CQDs on polyamide matrix membrane [24]. Moreover, Zhang et al. synthesised CQDs using citric acid and embedding them into a polyethyleneimine (PEI) matrix [20]. The results show that CQDs can operate as a nano-carrier through their functional groups and can be completely cross-linked with PEI [20]. The CQDs used in this study may possess several advantages, including their easy and low-cost production based on biomass material and green technology. These factors indicate the high application potential of CQDs as additives for membrane modification.

However, most polymer membranes are hydrophobic and are easily contaminated by microorganisms or bacteria, resulting in serious membrane fouling. The adherence of bacteria to solid surfaces occurs naturally where the attachment on the surfaces may cause the formation of biofilm [25]. Therefore, embedding CQDs on the polysulfone (PSF) membrane can be potentially effective and long-lasting in FO. CQDs exhibit intrinsic antibacterial properties because of the negatively charged carboxyl groups on their surfaces, causing electrostatic repulsion to occur on the membrane and bacterial surfaces. In general, bacteria have negatively charged particles on their cell surfaces because of the existing peptidoglycans in their cell walls [9,23]. In addition, CQDs can directly affect the cell bacteria on the membrane surface owing to their ultra-small size and large surface area. Zeng et al. applied CQDs from citric acid to a PES subtract surface with PDA consisting of amine groups creating a negatively charged solution that enhanced antibacterial properties [9]. A membrane with less fouling caused by microorganisms or bacterial adherence can have improved water permeability and reverse salt flux as a result of the excellent additional membrane properties, thus attracting more water molecules to the surface of the membrane [7].

In the present work, synthesised CQDs from oil palm biomass were used for the surface modification of PSF polymer membrane by developing a thin film composite with favourable CQD properties, including nanoscale size, high hydrophilicity, and antifouling and antibacterial properties. The nanoscale size, shape, and surface chemistry were determined, and their effects on dispersion in polar solvents and on polymeric substrate were further investigated in this study.

PSF granules were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Chemical Co., as the base polymer matrix with an average molecular weight of 35,000 g/mol. The polymer solvent of 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP; 99.5%) from Sigma-Aldrich Co. was used without purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbonisation-activation process of oil palm biomass
ce:section.ce:para: The method for obtaining the CQDs from oil palm biomass was similar to the method reported in our previous study [26]. Oil palm empty fruit bunches (OPEFBs) were dried at 105 °C for 24 h. This oil palm biomass was then carbonised and activated in a furnace. The dried OPEFBs were loaded onto a crucible, heated to 700 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min, and held for 2 h under N2 flow. The sample was allowed to cool to room temperature in an inert atmosphere. Then, the char (10 g) was placed into the furnace under N2 flow for 15 min prior to activation. The activation temperature was set to 800 °C under N2 flow until the N2 was switched to CO2. The activation temperature and CO2 flow rate were kept constant for 30 min, and then the sample was cooled down to room temperature under N2 flow.

CQDs were synthesised using activated carbon powder through hydrothermal treatment [26]. Approximately 2 g of activated carbon powder was added to 50 mL of deionised (DI) water. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 3 h. The solution was centrifuged, filtered using a 0.2 μm filter to remove the micron-sized particles, and stored at 4 °C. The CQDs were lyophilised and dispersed in water at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL.

PSF polymer resins were dried in an oven for 24 h at 50 °C to remove moisture. A polymer with 15% PSF by weight was prepared by dissolving 3 g of PSF in 17 g of NMP solvent and mixing with a low profile roller machine until the solution became homogeneous at room temperature. The polymer solution was poured and cast onto a glass plate using a casting knife to a thickness of 200 μm on an automatic casting machine at a constant speed. The glass plate was immersed in a coagulation bath containing DI water for approximately 5 min. The PSF membrane layer was immersed in a CQD solution. This procedure was performed for three membranes in three different solutions of CQD concentrations: 0.25, 0.38, and 0.5 mg/mL, with respect to the 0.5%, 0.75% and 1.0% concentration of CQDs, for 24 h to develop a thin layer composite CQDs on the PSF polymer membrane. The membranes were denoted as 0.5-CQDs, 0.75-CQDs, and 1.0-CQDs, respectively. Finally, the cured membrane was transferred to a fresh tray of DI water for membrane storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and membrane surface
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure of the CQDs was analysed by a transmission electron microscope (model JEM-2100F). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) samples were prepared by drop casting the CQD solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature before the analysis. The surface functional groups of the synthesised CQDs and PSF polymer membranes were analysed by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy using the FTIR spectrum (Perkin Elmer Spectrum 400 FT-IR) in transmission mode range of 400–4000 cm−1 with an accumulation of 32 scans and a resolution of 2.0 cm. For the element and compositional analysis of the CQDs and PSF polymer membrane, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was executed using a Kratos/AxisUltraDLD. The morphology of the membranes was evaluated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, PHILIPSXL30) measurements. The cross-section fractured membrane was visualised by cutting the membrane into small pieces and frozen with nitrogen liquid. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Park NX10) was used to analyse the surface roughness of membrane morphology.

A Sterlitech HP4750 stirred cell performed the analysis of the dead-end mode of the PSF and the CQDs-PSF membranes with a volume of 250 mL to measure the pure water flux J
w . The effective membrane area was 14.6 cm2. During the experiments, each membrane was pre-compacted at 10 bar for 1 h during the permeation tests. At each pressure, the pure water permeation was recorded for every 30 s and measured on an analytical balance. The membrane flux was calculated as follows:
(1)
J w
=
m
p ×
A m
× t
where m (mL) denotes the water mass collected during the time t (hr), ρ is the density of pure water (g/cm3) at room temperature and A
m is the effective membrane area (cm2) for water permeation. Pure water permeability (A) was determined by measuring the pure water flux, J
w (L/m2/h) at different trans-membrane pressures (TMP) from 2 bar to 20 bar; the slope of the linear regression of the water flux as a function of trans-membrane pressure was determined as the water permeability (A).

The synthesised CQDs-PSF membranes were then evaluated for FO applications. A cross-flow membrane cell (Sterlitech HP 4750) was used to perform the FO experiment with a total effective membrane area of 42 cm2. Two pumps with cross-flow velocity were used to circulate in a counter current mode of draw and feed solutions and maintained at 0.25 L/s. The draw and feed solution temperatures were at ambient condition. Samples were collected every 30 min in triplicate and averaged. The weight changes of the draw solution inside the tank were determined using a digital weighing balance placed under the draw solution tank, and the water flux was measured. A conductivity meter (Mettler-Toledo, model 300) monitored the conductivity of the sample. The deionised (DI) water was used as feed solution in FO experiment, whereas NaCl aqueous solution (1.5 M) was used as the draw solution. The water flux (J
w ) and reverse salt (J
s ) flux were calculated using the following Eqs. (2) and (3).
(2)
J w
=
∆ m
A m
∆ t
(3)
J s
=
∆
C t
V t
Jw
A m
∆t
where ∆m is the weight (g) change of the draw solution, A
m is the effective membrane area (cm2), C
t and V
t are the salt concentration (mol/L) and feed solution volume at the end of experiment, respectively, and ∆t is the measured time period (min).

The dry–wet weight method is used to calculated the membrane porosity. Membrane samples were cut (5 cm × 5 cm dimension) and soaked for 24 h in water. The samples membrane was dried at 40 °C in an oven. The wet and dry membranes were weighed using an analytical balance (Model Mattler Toledo). The thickness was measured using a digital Vernier calliper twice on the samples and all weights were recorded three times on an analytical balance. The porosity of the membrane was determined using the following equation (Eq. (4)):
(4)
Porosity =
Ww − Wd
ρAt
where W
w is the mass of wet membrane (g), W
d is the mass of dry membrane (g), ρ is the density of pure water (g/cm3), A is the active membrane surface area (cm2) and t is the thickness of membrane measured (μm).

The disk diffusion method was used to investigate the antibacterial activity of the membranes. Firstly, the Mueller–Hinton (MH) agar and nutrient broth (NB) were prepared to culture S. aureus and E. coli. Both bacteria were grown in an NB solution and incubated in a shaker incubator for overnight. The bacterial culture was checked for optical density (OD) to measure colony formation before the experiments were conducted. Then, 100 μL from each bacterium was spread onto an MH agar plate separately. Three sample disks were used: (1) PSF membrane, (2) CQDs and (3) CQDs-PSF membrane. Each was included in the control sample antibiotic for E. coli and S. aureus and placed on the MH agar plates. The sample disk was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, the inhibition zone diameter was measured and visually inspected around the sample disk.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on membrane intrinsic properties
ce:section.ce:para: The size of the CQD nanoparticles synthesised via hydrothermal treatment was measured by TEM spectroscopy. The TEM image of CQDs prepared by activated carbon derived from oil palm biomass with uniform dispersion shows a spherical shape (Fig. 1a). The histogram for size distribution was obtained by counting 200 particles as shown in Fig. 1b, and the average diameter was estimated to be approximately 5 ± 1 nm. The small size of CQDs can result in an improvement in the dispersion of CQDs particle in dope solutions and a membrane formation with a uniform structure [19].

FTIR analysis was carried out in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 to analyse the functional groups in the CQDs-PSF membrane. As shown in Fig. 1c, two spikes at 1672 and 3435 cm−1 correspond to the carboxyl (C=O) and hydroxyl (OH) groups, respectively. The presence of CO and OH groups indicates the presence of CQDs on the membrane surface. The CQD addition may result in enhanced surface hydrophilicity owing to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which can impart excellent water solubility and stability in an aqueous solution [6,19]. Furthermore, increasing surface hydrophilicity may improve the water permeability of membranes without a significant drop in rejection. Therefore, incorporating CQDs from biomass into the PSF matrix provided surface abilities for functionalization of CQDs and thus improved their stability and antifouling properties. The peak for the PSF membrane was assigned to the PSF group polymer membrane, showing the sulfone groups O=S=O at 1152 and 1242 cm−1, respectively.

The high-resolution XPS spectra for the CQDs-PSF membrane in Fig. 2a reveal the presence of elements C 1s and O 1s and a small concentration of S 2p from sulphur. The high-energy resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2b) can be curve-fitted into three peaks at 286.3, 287.2 and 289 eV, corresponding to CC, CO and CO, respectively. The O 1s and S 2p signals (Fig. 2c and d, respectively) can be curve-fitted into four peaks: 531 and 53 eV at CO and CO, while 167.5 and 168.8 eV at CS and C–SO2, respectively. The oxygen content is attributed to the surface oxidation of organic compounds on the surface of CQDs. The C 1s and O 1s peaks indicate that the carbon and oxygen in CQDs were successfully incorporated into the PSF membrane matrix. Furthermore, the high intensity of the C1s peak shows the abundant functional groups of carboxyl and hydroxyl on the surface, resulting in high hydrophilicity [10,26,27]. This phenomenon can be proven from the previous research stated that CQDs have shown hydrophilic properties owing to the presence of CO and CO which correspond to sp3 and sp2 carbons on the surface of CQDs [26]. Thus, the presence of hydrophilicity properties on membrane surfaces has contributed to a higher water permeability as well as increased the performance of FO processes. This hypothesis has been proven by Fathizadeh et al. [24] and Ma et al. [28] fabricated nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (N-GOQD) for the modification of the polyamide (PA) layer for reverse osmosis process. Both results show that the membrane has improved the water permeate flux without compromising salt rejection. This modification of TFC membranes gives membrane surfaces the presence of super hydrophilic nature and molecular sieving of nanomaterials [28].

The structural change of the PSF membrane was first analysed by SEM to observe the morphology transition at magnified images of 2000× as shown in Fig. 3a–d. These images present the cross-sectional area of the pristine PSF membrane (Fig. 3a) and the CQDs-PSF membranes (Fig. 3b–d). Generally, the CQDs-PSF membranes were observed to have long finger-like pores constructed across the membrane matrix. The SEM images of the membranes as presented in Fig. 3b–d show the asymmetric structure of the CQDs-PSF membranes consisting of a very thin dense skin layer on the top layer. Further investigation on the surface roughness of the active layer was carried out by using AFM and is discussed in the following Section 3.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permeability of water and solute using CQDs-PSF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The water flux and reverse salt flux for the PSF and CDQs-PSF membranes as a function of pressure difference across the membranes are shown in Fig. 4a. The water flux of the PSF membrane was lower than that of the CDQs-PDF membranes with high CQD concentrations (0.5%, 0.75%, and 1.0%). With increased pressure from 2 bar to 20 bar, 1.0-CQDs showed the highest water flux at 226 L/m2h at 20 bar. Similarly, the reverse salt fluxes of all the PSF and CQDs-PSF membranes increased with pressure. However, the reverse salt flux for CQDs-PSF membranes was 59.38 mol/m2h, which was higher than that of the unmodified PSF membrane at elevated pressure. This is due to the interaction of hydrophilic functionalized CQDs and PSF membrane, hence creating layers on the surface of the membrane and increasing the porosity of the FO membrane [11,29]. An increased porous structure of the membrane may decrease water resistance during transport, thereby allowing water to flow through the membrane easily. It is generally assumed that increasing hydrophilicity of a membrane surface will provide more opportunity for water, rather than foulants, to chemically associate with a membrane surface [29].

A comparison of the membrane performance in this study with other literature data is summarized in Table 1 . The pristine PSF membrane incorporated with silica nanoparticles and polyamide (PA)-SiO2 [30] has a water flux of 18.8 L/m2h with 43.45 mol/m2h of reverse salt flux at 0.6 MPa. For the membrane that was prepared by incorporating graphene oxide (GO) into PAA/PVA/GA, the water flux was 4.2 L/m2h at 0.5 MPa, while the reverse salt flux was 37.8 mol/m2h [31]. Comparing CQD modified tannic acid TFN membranes with other membranes from the literature, the former shows higher water flux coupled with lower reverse salt flux and lower pressure; the water flux was 1.5 times higher than that of the unmodified one which is 15.81 L/m2h [22]. The CQDs-PSF membrane used in this experiment, however, obtained higher water flux than that of the nanoparticles-modified membranes used by other researchers cited in this work; water flux approached 40 L/m2h under 0.2 MPa, with 13.21 mol/m2h of reverse salt flux. This indicates that the addition of CQDs has increased the membrane hydrophilicity more effectively, better than that of the other nanoparticles such as SiO2 and GO sheets, which is evident by the increased permeability in the permeation test as discussed and shown in Table 1.

The porosity of the polymeric membranes increased at the higher 1.0% CQD concentrations incorporated into the PSF membrane matrix as shown in Fig. 4c. The porosity of the 1.0-CQDs membranes was 64%, whereas that of the PSF membranes alone was only 56%. The increase of diffusion between solvent NMP and water is due to the presence of hydrophilic CQDs, causing the enhancement of porous structure of the modified membranes [10]. Generally, the functionalized CQDs play a major role in hydrophilicity behaviour in membrane surfaces affecting their permeability and fouling performance. The hydrophilic layer that formed on membrane surfaces can reduce the accumulation and adsorption of foulants and, therefore, improve membrane antifouling performance [7].

The 3D AFM images of the membranes are shown in Fig. 5 . The average surface roughness (Ra) and root mean square (Rms) of the membrane surface were determined using AFM and shown in Table 2 . Membrane-surface roughness is known to be one of the key factors affecting membrane-fouling resistance. A membrane with a uniformed and smooth surface has less tendency to fouling compared to one with high surface roughness. Foulants tend to absorb and accumulate on the surface of the membrane, causing a flux reduction [22]. The surface roughness analysis of the pristine PSF and CQDs-PSF membranes are presented in Table 2 and Fig. 5(a–d). The surface roughness of the CQDs-PSF membranes, less than the PSF membrane, decreases gradually with the increased concentration of CQDs. The Ra and Rms of the PSF membrane are 6.34 nm and 7.73 nm, respectively, while the 1.0% concentration of CQDs incorporated on the PSF membrane decreases these values to 1.87 nm and 2.56 nm, respectively. This reveals that the presence of nanoparticle CQDs resulting in a smoother morphology on the PSF membranes. Therefore, the smoother surface of the CQDs-PSF membranes would possess a better antifouling capability [22].

The antibacterial activities of CQDs-PSF membranes towards Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus were investigated by disk diffusion. The antibacterial activity of a membrane is an important indicator of its anti-biofouling performance. Fig. 6a and b shows the disk diffusion test of PSF and CQDs-PSF membranes, respectively, against E.coli and S. aureus, with the CQDs sample as a control. The PSF membrane had no inhibition zone, and bacteria were present around the membrane. However, a 10 mm inhibition zone around the disk of CQDs-PSF was observed, whereas no inhibition zone was observed for S. aureus. As such, the CQDs-PSF membranes exhibited resistance against Gram-negative E.coli. This is due to the resultant membrane with CQDs exhibiting enhanced surface hydrophilicity and great permeability with retention of salt selectivity, as shown in the permeation test in Section 3.2. Thus, the addition of CQDs improved their antibacterial properties. Moreover, the increased surface smoothness of the modified membranes, as shown in AFM result in Section 3.2, prevented the activity of bacteria and foulants on the membrane surfaces, improving the antifouling properties of the synthesised membrane. The presence of CQDs on the PSF membrane layer can effectively resist bacterial growth, thereby preventing the formation of biofilm on the membrane surface. A CQD surface is negatively charged because of the carboxyl and results in electrostatic repulsion against bacterial surfaces, which also have negative charges due to peptidoglycans in their cell wall [19].

In this study, a thin film composite CQDs-PSF membrane was successfully developed and tested in FO applications. CQDs-PSF significantly enhanced the membrane surface hydrophilicity and porosity, which improved membrane permeability. The highest water flux of 226 L/m2.h was obtained with a 1.0% CQD concentration with 59.38 mol/m2h of reverse salt flux. The modified CQDs-PSF membrane can potentially act as an antibacterial towards E. coli Gram-negative bacteria, thereby enhancing the antifouling performance of an FO membrane.
Nur Akma Mahat: Writing-Original draft preparation, Investigation, Formal analysis.
Siti Aisyah Shamsudin: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding Acquisition.
Nora Jullok: Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing, Formal analysis.
Akmal Hadi Ma'Radzi: Validation & Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the Research University Grant (GUP-2017-109) from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia and Fundamental Research Grant (FRGS/1/2018/TK05/UKM/02/3) from Ministry of Higher Education (Malaysia).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-incorporated thin film composite (TFC) membranes for forward osmosis (FO) desalination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xu, F. Li, B. Su, M.Z. Hu, X. Gao, C. Gao, Novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-incorporated thin film composite (TFC) membranes for forward osmosis (FO) desalination, Desalination. (2019) 219–230. doi:10.1016/j.desal.2018.04.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanotube membranes for water purification: a bright future in water desalination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Das, M.E. Ali, S.B.A. Hamid, S. Ramakrishna, Z.Z. Chowdhury, Carbon nanotube membranes for water purification: A bright future in water desalination, Desalination. 336 (2014) 97–109. doi:10.1016/j.desal.2013.12.026.
